title: Declare and assign
successMessage: Explicit type declarations document intent. int means a 32-bit integer, string means text.

title: Type inference with var
instruction:
`var` lets the compiler infer the type from the initial value.

The type is still fixed at compile time — just not written out.

Run this.
successMessage: var is not dynamic — it is static type inference. x is still an int, you just didn't write the word 'int'.

title: Reassigning variables
instruction:
Variables can change. Just use `=` (not `var` or type again).

Run this.
successMessage: += is shorthand for score = score + 25. C# also has -=, *=, /=, and %=.

title: Constants
instruction:
`const` creates a compile-time constant. The value can never change.

Naming convention: ALL_CAPS or PascalCase.

Run this.
successMessage: const is evaluated at compile time. It cannot be changed anywhere in the program.

title: Multiple assignments
instruction:
Declare multiple variables on separate lines or assign several at once.

Run this.
successMessage: You can declare multiple variables of the same type in one line. You can also declare first and assign later (x must be assigned before use).

title: Arithmetic operators
instruction:
Standard operators: `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`.

Note: integer division truncates.

Run this.
successMessage: 10 / 3 = 3 in integer division (truncated). Add a decimal: 10.0 / 3 = 3.333...

title: Increment and decrement
instruction:
`++` and `--` increment and decrement by 1.

Run this.
successMessage: ++ and -- are shorthand for += 1 and -= 1. Very common in loops.

title: Checking types with GetType
instruction:
Every variable has a type. Use `.GetType()` to inspect it at runtime.

Run this.
successMessage: var inferred int, double, string, bool. C# types are System.Int32, System.Double, etc. The short names are aliases.
